title: Detecting Cycles in a Graph: Fix the Low Link Values

statement.markdown: Given an unweighted graph represented as an adjacency list, detect if it contains any cycles using a low link value approach. The graph may be disconnected.
statement.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains two space-separated integers: n (the number of vertices) and m (the number of edges). The following m lines contain three space-separated integers: u, v, and w. Each edge is represented by a triplet (u, v, w), where u and v are the endpoints of the edge, and w is a dummy value (not used in this problem).
statement.outputFormat: The output should be a boolean value indicating whether the graph contains any cycles.

examples.explanation: The given graph contains a cycle.

hints.text: A cycle in a graph can be detected using a low link value approach.
Update the low link value for each vertex when its discovery time is set or when exploring its neighbors.

editorial.title: Detecting Cycles in a Graph
editorial.intuition: The low link value approach is used to detect cycles in a graph.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach involves checking every possible cycle in the graph.
editorial.betterSolution: A more efficient approach involves using the low link value approach.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses a depth-first search (DFS) with a low link value approach to detect cycles in the graph.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the low link value approach can be proven using a formal proof.
editorial.dryRun: A step-by-step example of the low link value approach can be used to understand the algorithm.
commonMistakes: Inaccurate updating of low link values
